ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mango sticky rice; FOODON_03311069 (glutinous rice food product) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (glutinous rice, cooked) is the closest parent; no composite dessert code for mango sticky rice exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for mango sticky rice or glutinous rice dessert as a clinical food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mango sticky rice is approximately 280 g, comprising a mound of coconut-infused glutinous rice (around 150 g), sliced fresh mango (around 100 g), and a drizzle of coconut cream sauce (around 30 g).

nutrient_source.title: Mango Sticky Rice — composite nutrient estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Glutinous rice, cooked (FDC ID 168872); Coconut milk, canned (FDC ID 170172); Mango, raw (FDC ID 169910); Sugar, granulated (FDC ID 169655)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for mango sticky rice as a composite dish. Values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for the four primary components: glutinous rice cooked (54% by weight), coconut milk canned (11%), fresh mango (36%), and granulated sugar (3%), then normalised to 100 g. Proportions are based on standard Thai recipe references cross-checked against HPB Singapore nutrient data for similar coconut-rice desserts. Energy calculated using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; glutinous rice (sticky rice) GI values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glutinous (sticky) rice has a published GI of approximately 59 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Mango sticky rice as a composite dish has no direct GI measurement; the GI of 59 is applied from the dominant carbohydrate component (glutinous rice), which constitutes approximately 54% of the dish by weight. The coconut milk fat content may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response, but no direct composite GI study was found. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (59 × 82.6) / 100 ≈ 48.7, where carbohydrate per 280 g serving = 29.5 × 2.8 = 82.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~55 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Mango sticky rice contains approximately 55 mg sodium per 100 g, making it a low-sodium dessert option that is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension when consumed in moderate portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving and high refined carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 59 and a glycemic load of ~48.7 per standard 280 g serving, mango sticky rice poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be consumed only occasionally in small portions with careful blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from coconut milk (~5.0 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The coconut milk component contributes approximately 5.0 g of saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption in the context of an overall saturated fat-controlled diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango Sticky Rice (Thai: ข้าวเหนียวมะม่วง, Khao Niao Mamuang; Chinese: 芒果糯米饭) is a classic Thai dessert that has become widely popular across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. It consists of glutinous (sticky) rice steamed until tender and then infused with sweetened coconut milk, served alongside slices of ripe fresh mango and finished with a drizzle of salted coconut cream. The texture is soft, chewy, and richly creamy, with a natural sweetness from both the coconut milk and the mango. The flavour profile balances the floral sweetness of ripe mango against the savoury-sweet coconut rice. Nutritionally, a standard 280 g restaurant serving provides approximately 490 kcal, dominated by carbohydrates (~82.6 g) from the glutinous rice and sugars from the mango and added sugar. Saturated fat (~14 g per serving) comes primarily from the coconut milk. The dish is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. From a clinical perspective, the high glycemic load (~48.7 per serving) makes it a high-concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The moderate saturated fat content warrants attention for those managing cardiovascular disease. Sodium is low (~154 mg per serving), making it suitable for hypertension management when portion-controlled. The mango component contributes vitamin C (~26.6 mg per serving) and vitamin A (~78.4 mcg RAE per serving). This dish is commonly served as a dessert or snack and is available year-round in Singapore at Thai restaurants, hawker stalls, and food delivery platforms.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for mango sticky rice as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central component entries (glutinous rice, coconut milk, mango, sugar). GI is derived from the analogous glutinous rice GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients; no single approved-source entry for mango sticky rice as a prepared dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA at time of processing. A direct HPB or FAO entry, if identified in future, should supersede these estimates and confidence should be upgraded to high. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for all four component ingredients; coconut-derived saturated fat is not a trans fat. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; all ingredients are plant-derived. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for all component ingredients; no fortification assumed. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.3) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1) are estimated from USDA component data. 7. The dish is listed under 'Singapore' cuisine in the source field survey record but is of Thai origin; both are noted in region_of_origin and cuisine fields. 8. The GI of 59 places this dish in the medium GI category (56–69 per Veritas threshold rules). 9. Serving size of 280 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portions observed in Singapore; no standardised serving size was found in an approved source. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent match types as no exact composite-dish codes exist for mango sticky rice in FoodOn or FoodEx2.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Assigned canonical food_name 'Mango Sticky Rice'. Computed composite nutrient estimates from USDA FoodData Central component entries. Applied GI 59 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous glutinous rice entry. Populated all mandatory Singaporean-cuisine multilingual fields (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Thai given dish origin. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Confirmed all zero values (trans_fat, cholesterol, vitamin_b12, vitamin_d) are documented in notes. Confirmed all -1 sentinel values are replaced with estimated numeric values where possible from USDA data. Verified GI category 'medium' is correct for GI=59 per Veritas threshold (56–69=medium). Verified ontology match_type values use only allowed strings (closest_parent, none). Record scored 8/8 on first iteration.
